narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 49% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to flag your profile as a risk. Your background includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, well below the typical 5.71 average, a Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the usual 5.53, middle family income around the dataset's 3.47 average, an LSAT score of 36 slightly under the 36.7 norm, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.7 versus the standard 3.21. As a Black female planning full-time studies, you're in underrepresented groups—Black students make up just 4.6% of the dataset, females 43.6%—while full-time enrollment aligns with the 92.7% majority. The model's decision hinged most on your low Year 1 and Year 3 deciles, which were the top two influences pushing toward a failure prediction since they're far below what successful bar passers typically show. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, also nudging the outlook negative due to being a bit low. Family income came next at fourth, followed by race at fifth, undergraduate GPA at sixth, gender at seventh, and full-time status last with minimal sway—all slightly contributing to the risk assessment in your case. Compared to typical students who pass, your early and later school rankings stand out as the biggest departures, signaling potential challenges in sustaining high performance. That 49% failure probability means you're on the edge, but these academic metrics tipped the scale against approval. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 37, build stronger study habits to mimic top decile performers, and seek bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students. Gaining practical experience or supplemental coursework could also demonstrate growth beyond your past grades. With targeted effort, you can shift these factors and reapply stronger—many have turned similar profiles around. Keep pushing; your determination is already a great start.
